audioscript.text: Section 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will hear a conversation between two friends who are planning to visit a friend of theirs who has just had a baby.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 1 to 5.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will see that there is an example which has been done for you.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: On this occasion only, the conversation relating to this will be played first.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: John, I've just had some good news.
audioscript.speaker: SARAH

audioscript.text: Susan has had her baby.
audioscript.speaker: SARAH

audioscript.text: Do you know when she had it?
audioscript.speaker: JOHN

audioscript.text: Yesterday, the tenth of August.
audioscript.speaker: SARAH

audioscript.text: Oh.
audioscript.speaker: JOHN

audioscript.text: My father was born on August the 10th.
audioscript.speaker: JOHN

audioscript.text: The baby was born on the tenth of August.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: So, August 10th has been written on the form.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now we shall begin.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You should answer the questions as you listen, because you will not hear the recording a second time.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Listen carefully to the first part of the conversation, and answer questions 1 to 5.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: John, I've just had some good news.
audioscript.speaker: SARAH

audioscript.text: Susan has had her baby.
audioscript.speaker: SARAH

audioscript.text: Do you know when she had it?
audioscript.speaker: JOHN

audioscript.text: Yesterday, the 10th of August.
audioscript.speaker: SARAH

audioscript.text: Oh.
audioscript.speaker: JOHN

audioscript.text: My father was born on August the 10th.
audioscript.speaker: JOHN

audioscript.text: Give me the details, and I'll make a note for everyone at work.
audioscript.speaker: JOHN

audioscript.text: OK.
audioscript.speaker: SARAH

audioscript.text: Well, was it a boy or a girl?
audioscript.speaker: JOHN

audioscript.text: It's a boy.
audioscript.speaker: SARAH

audioscript.text: And what are they gonna call him?
audioscript.speaker: JOHN

audioscript.text: Tom Lightfoot.
audioscript.speaker: SARAH

audioscript.text: That sounds quite good, don't you think?
audioscript.speaker: SARAH

audioscript.text: Yes, that has quite a good ring to it.
audioscript.speaker: JOHN

audioscript.text: You know he's quite a big baby.
audioscript.speaker: SARAH

audioscript.text: He weighed four and a quarter kilos when he was born.
audioscript.speaker: SARAH

audioscript.text: Oh, that does sound big, four and a quarter kilos.
audioscript.speaker: JOHN

audioscript.text: And he's long too, 46 centimeters.
audioscript.speaker: SARAH

audioscript.text: Hmm, tall parents.
audioscript.speaker: JOHN

audioscript.text: He'll grow up to be over 2 meters I'd say.
audioscript.speaker: JOHN

audioscript.text: With masses of black hair, curly black hair.
audioscript.speaker: SARAH

audioscript.text: You know, we should go and visit them in hospital.
audioscript.speaker: SARAH

audioscript.text: What about tomorrow afternoon at around 1 pm?
audioscript.speaker: SARAH

audioscript.text: Yes, OK.
audioscript.speaker: JOHN

audioscript.text: Where should we meet?
audioscript.speaker: SARAH

audioscript.text: Ah, I could come and pick you up at your house if you like.
audioscript.speaker: SARAH

audioscript.text: Yes, that would be wonderful.
audioscript.speaker: JOHN

audioscript.text: My car is still off the road.
audioscript.speaker: JOHN

audioscript.text: Just refresh my memory, uh, what's the address again?
audioscript.speaker: SARAH

audioscript.text: It's 15 Chesterfield Road, Paddington.
audioscript.speaker: JOHN

audioscript.text: It's next to the library, isn't it?
audioscript.speaker: SARAH

audioscript.text: Uh. Not exactly, it's next to a bank.
audioscript.speaker: JOHN

audioscript.text: The State Bank actually. The library is opposite us, on the corner.
audioscript.speaker: JOHN

audioscript.text: That's right, and there's a garage on the other street corner, I remember now.
audioscript.speaker: SARAH

audioscript.text: Hmm.
audioscript.speaker: SARAH

audioscript.text: So you'll pick me up at a quarter to 1, and we'll be there at 1 easily.
audioscript.speaker: JOHN

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the conversation, you have some time to look at questions 6 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 6 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now, what should we take?
audioscript.speaker: SARAH

audioscript.text: We must take them something.
audioscript.speaker: SARAH

audioscript.text: I always think flowers are good to take to someone in hospital, don't you?
audioscript.speaker: JOHN

audioscript.text: Well, not really.
audioscript.speaker: SARAH

audioscript.text: Everyone always brings flowers, and they don't last.
audioscript.speaker: SARAH

audioscript.text: I think it's much better to take a pot plant, so she can take it home with her.
audioscript.speaker: SARAH

audioscript.text: Yes, but then she has to remember to water it.
audioscript.speaker: JOHN

audioscript.text: And what about a big box of chocolates?
audioscript.speaker: JOHN

audioscript.text: OK, chocolates sound fine.
audioscript.speaker: SARAH

audioscript.text: We should get something for the baby too.
audioscript.speaker: SARAH

audioscript.text: What do you think?
audioscript.speaker: SARAH

audioscript.text: Yes, you're right.
audioscript.speaker: JOHN

audioscript.text: What do you think of something like baby shampoo or talcum powder?
audioscript.speaker: JOHN

audioscript.text: Or we could get a little hat or something like that.
audioscript.speaker: SARAH

audioscript.text: We don't know the size, or the right color, do we?
audioscript.speaker: JOHN

audioscript.text: I think we should get something they wouldn't normally buy.
audioscript.speaker: SARAH

audioscript.text: What about a soft toy of some sort?
audioscript.speaker: SARAH

audioscript.text: Yes, a soft toy.
audioscript.speaker: JOHN

audioscript.text: What about a teddy bear?
audioscript.speaker: SARAH

audioscript.text: I could get one early tomorrow at the market.
audioscript.speaker: JOHN

audioscript.text: And I could probably get the chocolates there too.
audioscript.speaker: JOHN

audioscript.text: Good.
audioscript.speaker: SARAH

audioscript.text: So you'll pick me up at a quarter to 1 at my place, and I'll make sure that I've got the presents.
audioscript.speaker: JOHN

audioscript.text: You must remember how much you paid for the gifts.
audioscript.speaker: SARAH

audioscript.text: So I can pay you back for half, if they're going to be from both of us, I would like to go shares.
audioscript.speaker: SARAH

audioscript.text: OK, I'd say the chocolates would be about $15 for something nice, and not too small.
audioscript.speaker: JOHN

audioscript.text: And the toy would be around $35 or so, I think.
audioscript.speaker: JOHN

audioscript.text: Good, that'll be fine.
audioscript.speaker: SARAH

audioscript.text: About $25 each then.
audioscript.speaker: SARAH

audioscript.text: Good, I'll pick you up then on Sunday at 12:45.
audioscript.speaker: SARAH

audioscript.text: OK.
audioscript.speaker: JOHN

audioscript.text: See you then.
audioscript.speaker: SARAH

audioscript.text: Bye.
audioscript.speaker: SARAH

audioscript.text: That is the end of section 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

test_text.title: Household Gadgets at the Good Home Exhibition

audioscript.text: Section 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will hear a popular science radio program.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Liz Shearer talks about some new devices for use in the home.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 11 to 16.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully to the radio program, and answer questions 11 to 16.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Good evening. Tonight's show comes to you from the Good Home Exhibition in Duke's Court, where we've been trying out some of the latest gadgets on show here, and getting our resident expert, Liz Shearer, to tell us which ones are worth buying, and which will die a death.
audioscript.speaker: PRESENTER

audioscript.text: Well, hello.
audioscript.speaker: LIZ SHEARER

audioscript.text: Yes, John, I've been investigating 4 new household gadgets, and sorting out the advantages and disadvantages.
audioscript.speaker: LIZ SHEARER

audioscript.text: And then really deciding what are must buys, what are maybe buys, and what are never buys.
audioscript.speaker: LIZ SHEARER

audioscript.text: Ha, let's start with this vacuum flask for keeping drinks hot.
audioscript.speaker: LIZ SHEARER

audioscript.text: Well, I felt this had quite a lot going for it.
audioscript.speaker: LIZ SHEARER

audioscript.text: Most of all is the fact that it contains no glass, and is therefore unbreakable to all intents and purposes.
audioscript.speaker: LIZ SHEARER

audioscript.text: It's made of stainless steel which is guaranteed for 20 years.
audioscript.speaker: LIZ SHEARER

audioscript.text: Hope that's long enough.
audioscript.speaker: LIZ SHEARER

audioscript.text: And it's true what the manufacturer claims - that it does maintain heat for 18 hours.
audioscript.speaker: LIZ SHEARER

audioscript.text: So that's pretty good.
audioscript.speaker: LIZ SHEARER

audioscript.text: On the downside, it really works out to be quite expensive, and much more surprisingly, it unfortunately leaves a strange taste, you know when you've drunk from it.
audioscript.speaker: LIZ SHEARER

audioscript.text: So, all in all, my recommendation would be it's got plenty of advantages, but it is rather expensive.
audioscript.speaker: LIZ SHEARER

audioscript.text: So, I'd say you should maybe buy it.
audioscript.speaker: LIZ SHEARER

audioscript.text: Moving on to a natty little device, the whistle key holder.
audioscript.speaker: LIZ SHEARER

audioscript.text: Basically, this is where you whistle, and the key holder gives off a high pitched noise and flashes light.
audioscript.speaker: LIZ SHEARER

audioscript.text: So you can find it.
audioscript.speaker: LIZ SHEARER

audioscript.text: One advantage of this model is that it also has a small light.
audioscript.speaker: LIZ SHEARER

audioscript.text: You press the button, and this means you can find keyholes easily.
audioscript.speaker: LIZ SHEARER

audioscript.text: I also felt the small size was a real advantage. On the weaker side, I did find the noise unpleasant, which I'm sure the designers could have done something about.
audioscript.speaker: LIZ SHEARER

audioscript.text: And I found that it didn't work through metal, so it's mainly useful for finding in coat pockets, cushions, et cetera.
audioscript.speaker: LIZ SHEARER

audioscript.text: But taken as a whole, I thought it was a masterpiece of design, and would highly recommend it.
audioscript.speaker: LIZ SHEARER

audioscript.text: Now you have some time to look at questions 17 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 17 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: The third gizmo is called the Army Flashlight, because it was developed initially for military use.
audioscript.speaker: LIZ SHEARER

audioscript.text: It works by squeezing the handle to generate the power.
audioscript.speaker: LIZ SHEARER

audioscript.text: Its advantages are that it can be used for outside activities, and also, and this is one of the surprising features, it does work underwater.
audioscript.speaker: LIZ SHEARER

audioscript.text: My main objection to it though was, although it did work in these conditions, this model gave off a weak light.
audioscript.speaker: LIZ SHEARER

audioscript.text: So my recommendation I'm afraid would have to be to avoid this one. The decoy camera was last on my list.
audioscript.speaker: LIZ SHEARER

audioscript.text: This is a fake video camera which you fix to your wall to scare off burglars.
audioscript.speaker: LIZ SHEARER

audioscript.text: The advantage of this model is something which makes it look very realistic, it's flashing light.
audioscript.speaker: LIZ SHEARER

audioscript.text: On the downside, it was quite difficult to fix to the wall, however.
audioscript.speaker: LIZ SHEARER

audioscript.text: Burglary is such a major problem these days that it is worth the effort.
audioscript.speaker: LIZ SHEARER

audioscript.text: So, this gets my strong recommendation.
audioscript.speaker: LIZ SHEARER

audioscript.text: OK, thanks for that, Liz.
audioscript.speaker: PRESENTER

audioscript.text: And now I'd like...
audioscript.speaker: PRESENTER

audioscript.text: That is the end of section 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: So my recommendation I'm afraid would have to be to avoid this one. The decoy camera was last on my list.
utterances.speaker: LIZ SHEARER

utterances.text: This is a fake video camera which you fix to your wall to scare off burglars.
utterances.speaker: LIZ SHEARER

utterances.text: The advantage of this model is something which makes it look very realistic, it's flashing light.
utterances.speaker: LIZ SHEARER

utterances.text: On the downside, it was quite difficult to fix to the wall, however.
utterances.speaker: LIZ SHEARER

utterances.text: Burglary is such a major problem these days that it is worth the effort.
utterances.speaker: LIZ SHEARER

utterances.text: So, this gets my strong recommendation.
utterances.speaker: LIZ SHEARER

utterances.text: OK, thanks for that, Liz.
utterances.speaker: PRESENTER

utterances.text: And now I'd like...
utterances.speaker: PRESENTER

utterances.text: That is the end of section 2.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You will now have half a minute to check your answers.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

items: Press-button light useful for finding keyhole
instruction: For the "Recommendation" column, write A, B or C:
B. Maybe you should buy this.
C. You should never buy this.

answer_key.correct_answer: taste | strange taste | a strange taste
small size | the small size | small | size | the size

audioscript.text: Section 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Listen to a female student, Amina, talking to her tutor, doctor Bryson, about her project on local history.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Amina has completed a first draft of the project.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 21 to 26.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully to the first part of the discussion, and answer questions 21 to 26.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Well, Amina, thanks for letting me have your draft in such good time.
audioscript.speaker: BRYSON

audioscript.text: Oh, that's all right.
audioscript.speaker: AMINA

audioscript.text: I was just very anxious to hear what you think of it.
audioscript.speaker: AMINA

audioscript.text: You can see that I decided to change the topic.
audioscript.speaker: AMINA

audioscript.text: I had been interested in looking at Barings Factory.
audioscript.speaker: AMINA

audioscript.text: Oh, I think the hospital was a much better choice.
audioscript.speaker: BRYSON

audioscript.text: In fact, well, I have to say that I thought it was good.
audioscript.speaker: BRYSON

audioscript.text: Oh.
audioscript.speaker: AMINA

audioscript.text: There's still lots of work to be done.
audioscript.speaker: BRYSON

audioscript.text: Oh, yes, of course.
audioscript.speaker: AMINA

audioscript.text: But there's plenty of good ideas.
audioscript.speaker: BRYSON

audioscript.text: It opens well, and the first chapter is fine, but the middle section really stood out for me, most interesting.
audioscript.speaker: BRYSON

audioscript.text: That's amazing, because I really didn't find it a bit easy to write.
audioscript.speaker: AMINA

audioscript.text: How long did you work on the whole thing?
audioscript.speaker: BRYSON

audioscript.text: Well, I spent about two or 3 weeks reading and doing general research, and then I dashed the writing off very quickly, so about 4 weeks in all.
audioscript.speaker: AMINA

audioscript.text: Well, that's about par for the course.
audioscript.speaker: BRYSON

audioscript.text: You've got a while yet to make the changes.
audioscript.speaker: BRYSON

audioscript.text: Oh, right, no problem.
audioscript.speaker: AMINA

audioscript.text: Right, let's have a look at my notes here.
audioscript.speaker: BRYSON

audioscript.text: OK, starting with section headings, so the broad divisions are good, but you'll have to redo the actual headings.
audioscript.speaker: BRYSON

audioscript.text: I've made some suggestions in the margins.
audioscript.speaker: BRYSON

audioscript.text: OK, thanks.
audioscript.speaker: AMINA

audioscript.text: Now this information on local housing.
audioscript.speaker: BRYSON

audioscript.text: I can see why you put it there, but it really isn't relevant to the approach you've taken.
audioscript.speaker: BRYSON

audioscript.text: I think I see what you mean.
audioscript.speaker: AMINA

audioscript.text: Now, what did I say about the interviews?
audioscript.speaker: BRYSON

audioscript.text: Oh, I worked very hard on those.
audioscript.speaker: AMINA

audioscript.text: I really thought they were valuable.
audioscript.speaker: AMINA

audioscript.text: They are, Amina, but they're very complex and rather unclear at the moment.
audioscript.speaker: BRYSON

audioscript.text: You're gonna have to spend a bit of time making the data a lot clearer.
audioscript.speaker: BRYSON

audioscript.text: OK, as long as I don't have to remove them altogether.
audioscript.speaker: AMINA

audioscript.text: No, don't worry.
audioscript.speaker: BRYSON

audioscript.text: What about the chronology, the list of dates?
audioscript.speaker: AMINA

audioscript.text: I wasn't sure whether I should rewrite those.
audioscript.speaker: AMINA

audioscript.text: My advice on that is to take them out.
audioscript.speaker: BRYSON

audioscript.text: I feel it makes the whole piece appear too simplistic.
audioscript.speaker: BRYSON

audioscript.text: OK, if it'll help.
audioscript.speaker: AMINA

audioscript.text: Now you have some time to look at questions 27 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen to the rest of the discussion and answer questions 27 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now, there are a couple of other books I'd like you to look at.
audioscript.speaker: BRYSON

audioscript.text: Have you got a pen?
audioscript.speaker: BRYSON

audioscript.text: Yeah.
audioscript.speaker: AMINA

audioscript.text: Right, Approaches to Local History by John Mervis.
audioscript.speaker: BRYSON

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: AMINA

audioscript.text: And then I think you need to think about ways of representing interview data, have a look at Sight and Sound by Kate Oakwell.
audioscript.speaker: BRYSON

audioscript.text: Sight and Sound.
audioscript.speaker: AMINA

audioscript.text: Then you know I'm going away on holiday next week.
audioscript.speaker: BRYSON

audioscript.text: Yes.
audioscript.speaker: AMINA

audioscript.text: So when you've made the changes I suggest you show the work to your support tutor.
audioscript.speaker: BRYSON

audioscript.text: Support tutor, right.
audioscript.speaker: AMINA

audioscript.text: Then you do the proofreading.
audioscript.speaker: BRYSON

audioscript.text: Proofreading.
audioscript.speaker: AMINA

audioscript.text: Uh huh.
audioscript.speaker: AMINA

audioscript.text: When by, do you think?
audioscript.speaker: AMINA

audioscript.text: I'd aim for 29th of June.
audioscript.speaker: BRYSON

audioscript.text: And after that you should get it laser printed, but be careful because the computer center closes on the tenth of July.
audioscript.speaker: BRYSON

audioscript.text: Hmm. And then I hand it in to...?
audioscript.speaker: AMINA

audioscript.text: Oh, the Faculty Office as usual.
audioscript.speaker: BRYSON

audioscript.text: OK, that's fine.
audioscript.speaker: AMINA

audioscript.text: I think I'm all set now.
audioscript.speaker: AMINA

audioscript.text: Thanks very much for all your help.
audioscript.speaker: AMINA

audioscript.text: A pleasure.
audioscript.speaker: BRYSON

audioscript.text: See you when I get back.
audioscript.speaker: BRYSON

audioscript.text: Yep, thanks, Dr Bryson.
audioscript.speaker: AMINA

audioscript.text: Bye.
audioscript.speaker: AMINA

audioscript.text: Bye.
audioscript.speaker: BRYSON

audioscript.text: That is the end of section 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

test_text.title: Environmental Noise at a Planned Housing Estate

audioscript.text: Section 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will hear a talk given by a university lecturer about the impact of environmental noise on a proposed building site.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Before you hear the talk, you have some time to look at questions 31 to 35. Now listen carefully to the first part of the talk, and answer questions 31 to 35.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Good afternoon.
audioscript.speaker: PAULA BUNDELL

audioscript.text: I'm Paula Bundell, and I am giving you the lectures on environmental noise this term.
audioscript.speaker: PAULA BUNDELL

audioscript.text: Today, we're going to look into the effects of noise on a planned housing estate.
audioscript.speaker: PAULA BUNDELL

audioscript.text: In a particularly difficult part of the new Manchester Park area.
audioscript.speaker: PAULA BUNDELL

audioscript.text: This site is not as bad as some I have researched in the past.
audioscript.speaker: PAULA BUNDELL

audioscript.text: The Blacktown Airport is closed from 6 pm to 7 am.
audioscript.speaker: PAULA BUNDELL

audioscript.text: And this is a great advantage to the site.
audioscript.speaker: PAULA BUNDELL

audioscript.text: The only noise after dark is from the highway, and the traffic is somewhat reduced between 7:30 pm and 5:30 am so the people most affected by the noise will be I expect housewives.
audioscript.speaker: PAULA BUNDELL

audioscript.text: By the time most of the students and workers have arrived back home in the evening during the week.
audioscript.speaker: PAULA BUNDELL

audioscript.text: The noise will have abated to a fairly large extent.
audioscript.speaker: PAULA BUNDELL

audioscript.text: The weekends are still a problem of course, but the traffic is certainly reduced on Saturdays to a large extent.
audioscript.speaker: PAULA BUNDELL

audioscript.text: And even more so on Sundays.
audioscript.speaker: PAULA BUNDELL

audioscript.text: Of course, modifications to houses will be necessary at a site like this, and they come at a significant cost to the developer and home buyer.
audioscript.speaker: PAULA BUNDELL

audioscript.text: The modifications I'm about to outline will add about $25,000 to the price of a newly built house.
audioscript.speaker: PAULA BUNDELL

audioscript.text: That will still mean a cheaper house than in a less noisy and more desirable area.
audioscript.speaker: PAULA BUNDELL

audioscript.text: A bit of background would not go astray.
audioscript.speaker: PAULA BUNDELL

audioscript.text: I understand that you are all familiar with the proposed development site at Manchester Park.
audioscript.speaker: PAULA BUNDELL

audioscript.text: It's a particularly difficult one in terms of noise, with the highway along the eastern perimeter, and the Blacktown airport not 3 km away to the north.
audioscript.speaker: PAULA BUNDELL

audioscript.text: Of course, those nearest the highway will be the worst hit, with heavy traffic noise as well as the noise from the light planes overhead.
audioscript.speaker: PAULA BUNDELL

audioscript.text: As you all know, the normal noise threshold for private housing is 55 decibels.
audioscript.speaker: PAULA BUNDELL

audioscript.text: At this site, the levels have been recorded as high as 67 decibels.
audioscript.speaker: PAULA BUNDELL

audioscript.text: Before the talk continues, you have some time to look at questions 36 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now as the talk continues, answer questions 36 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: The construction of the houses has to be somewhat modified from houses in most areas.
audioscript.speaker: PAULA BUNDELL

audioscript.text: In the houses on the highway, and in the noisiest areas of this site, there will be a need for specialized double glazing.
audioscript.speaker: PAULA BUNDELL

audioscript.text: And special acoustic seals will have to be fitted to the doors.
audioscript.speaker: PAULA BUNDELL

audioscript.text: All exterior doors in this especially noisy pocket will have to be solid core wood doors with hinges.
audioscript.speaker: PAULA BUNDELL

audioscript.text: Every house built on this site, uh, not just those adjacent to the highway or nearest to the airport, will require high density insulation materials in the roof.
audioscript.speaker: PAULA BUNDELL

audioscript.text: Not only will all the roofs need insulating, the exterior walls will be required to be double brick.
audioscript.speaker: PAULA BUNDELL

audioscript.text: All ceilings will require double thickness plasterboard to be used in the construction.
audioscript.speaker: PAULA BUNDELL

audioscript.text: In the noisiest areas, mechanical ventilation will have to be installed in the exterior walls.
audioscript.speaker: PAULA BUNDELL

audioscript.text: In those areas with sealed windows, it will be necessary to fit fans with absorbers to cut out the noise in those particular houses.
audioscript.speaker: PAULA BUNDELL

audioscript.text: Air conditioning units could also be fitted in the ceilings of such houses, but this is substantially more expensive than fans, and may not be needed on this site.
audioscript.speaker: PAULA BUNDELL

audioscript.text: Uh. Coming back now to the double glazing I mentioned before.
audioscript.speaker: PAULA BUNDELL

audioscript.text: Uh. Specialized double glazing requires a larger air gap between the inner and outer glass than normal double glazing.
audioscript.speaker: PAULA BUNDELL

audioscript.text: The gap must be at least 7 cm.
audioscript.speaker: PAULA BUNDELL

audioscript.text: The thickness of the glass is also a factor, 8 millimeters on the outside, and 6 on the inside pane.
audioscript.speaker: PAULA BUNDELL

audioscript.text: It's essential that the glass be thicker on the outside than on the inside, and that the gap between the panes of glass be a minimum of 7 cm.
audioscript.speaker: PAULA BUNDELL

audioscript.text: Obviously, the noise factor will have to be taken into consideration with the layout of the houses.
audioscript.speaker: PAULA BUNDELL

audioscript.text: Living areas will have to be designed at the back of the houses, away from the highway.
audioscript.speaker: PAULA BUNDELL

audioscript.text: Bedrooms and living rooms will have to be built towards the back, and for those houses closest to the highway, two layers of plasterboard will be needed for the interior bedroom walls.
audioscript.speaker: PAULA BUNDELL

audioscript.text: Those rooms constructed at the front of the houses should be garages, laundries, kitchens, bathrooms, and dining rooms.
audioscript.speaker: PAULA BUNDELL

audioscript.text: I have come to the conclusion that this development should go ahead.
audioscript.speaker: PAULA BUNDELL

audioscript.text: But with various acoustic modifications, according to the position of the block in relation to the highway and intersection.
audioscript.speaker: PAULA BUNDELL

audioscript.text: That is the end of Section 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: All exterior doors in this especially noisy pocket will have to be solid core wood doors with hinges.
utterances.speaker: PAULA BUNDELL

utterances.text: Every house built on this site, uh, not just those adjacent to the highway or nearest to the airport, will require high density insulation materials in the roof.
utterances.speaker: PAULA BUNDELL

utterances.text: Not only will all the roofs need insulating, the exterior walls will be required to be double brick.
utterances.speaker: PAULA BUNDELL

utterances.text: All ceilings will require double thickness plasterboard to be used in the construction.
utterances.speaker: PAULA BUNDELL

utterances.text: In the noisiest areas, mechanical ventilation will have to be installed in the exterior walls.
utterances.speaker: PAULA BUNDELL

utterances.text: In those areas with sealed windows, it will be necessary to fit fans with absorbers to cut out the noise in those particular houses.
utterances.speaker: PAULA BUNDELL

utterances.text: Air conditioning units could also be fitted in the ceilings of such houses, but this is substantially more expensive than fans, and may not be needed on this site.
utterances.speaker: PAULA BUNDELL

utterances.text: Uh. Coming back now to the double glazing I mentioned before.
utterances.speaker: PAULA BUNDELL

utterances.text: Uh. Specialized double glazing requires a larger air gap between the inner and outer glass than normal double glazing.
utterances.speaker: PAULA BUNDELL

utterances.text: The gap must be at least 7 cm.
utterances.speaker: PAULA BUNDELL

utterances.text: The thickness of the glass is also a factor, 8 millimeters on the outside, and 6 on the inside pane.
utterances.speaker: PAULA BUNDELL

utterances.text: It's essential that the glass be thicker on the outside than on the inside, and that the gap between the panes of glass be a minimum of 7 cm.
utterances.speaker: PAULA BUNDELL

utterances.text: Obviously, the noise factor will have to be taken into consideration with the layout of the houses.
utterances.speaker: PAULA BUNDELL

utterances.text: Living areas will have to be designed at the back of the houses, away from the highway.
utterances.speaker: PAULA BUNDELL

utterances.text: Bedrooms and living rooms will have to be built towards the back, and for those houses closest to the highway, two layers of plasterboard will be needed for the interior bedroom walls.
utterances.speaker: PAULA BUNDELL

utterances.text: Those rooms constructed at the front of the houses should be garages, laundries, kitchens, bathrooms, and dining rooms.
utterances.speaker: PAULA BUNDELL

utterances.text: I have come to the conclusion that this development should go ahead.
utterances.speaker: PAULA BUNDELL

utterances.text: But with various acoustic modifications, according to the position of the block in relation to the highway and intersection.
utterances.speaker: PAULA BUNDELL

utterances.text: That is the end of Section 4.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You will now have half a minute to check your answers.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER
